message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

After a regular checkpoint the following log warning is writte nfor all nodes:  [WARNING]  walCheckpoint.go:343: [METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation skipped for var/metric-checkpoints/fritz/f0748 (channel full)
Mar 28 06:39:43 monitoring cc-backend[45645]: [WARNING]  walCheckpoint.go:343: [METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation skipped for var/metric-checkpoints/fritz/f2263 (channel full)
Mar 28 06:39:43 monitoring cc-backend[45645]: [WARNING]  walCheckpoint.go:343: [METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation skipped for var/metric-checkpoints/fritz/f0481 (channel full)
Mar 28 06:39:43 monitoring cc-backend[45645]: [WARNING]  walCheckpoint.go:343: [METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation skipped for var/metric-checkpoints/fritz/f0708 (channel full)
Mar 28 06:39:43 monitoring cc-backend[45645]: [WARNING]  walCheckpoint.go:343: [METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation skipped for var/metric-checkpoints/fritz/f0373 (channel full) . Investigate and issue and suggest fixes.

1	// Copyright (C) NHR@FAU, University Erlangen-Nuremberg.
2	// All rights reserved. This file is part of cc-backend.
3	// Use of this source code is governed by a MIT-style
4	// license that can be found in the LICENSE file.
5	
6	// Package metricstore provides walCheckpoint.go: WAL-based checkpoint implementation.
7	//
8	// This replaces the Avro shadow tree with an append-only Write-Ahead Log (WAL)
9	// per host, eliminating the extra memory overhead of the AvroStore and providing
10	// truly continuous (per-write) crash safety.
11	//
12	// # Architecture
13	//
14	//	Metric write (DecodeLine)
15	//	      │
16	//	      ├─► WriteToLevel()    → main MemoryStore (unchanged)
17	//	      │
18	//	      └─► WALMessages channel
19	//	               │
20	//	               ▼
21	//	        WALStaging goroutine
22	//	               │
23	//	               ▼
24	//	        checkpoints/cluster/host/current.wal  (append-only, binary)
25	//
26	//	Periodic checkpoint (Checkpointing goroutine):
27	//	  1. Write <timestamp>.bin snapshot (column-oriented, from main tree)
28	//	  2. Signal WALStaging to truncate current.wal per host
29	//
30	//	On restart (FromCheckpoint):
31	//	  1. Load most recent <timestamp>.bin snapshot
32	//	  2. Replay current.wal (overwrite-safe: buffer.write handles duplicate timestamps)
33	//
34	// # WAL Record Format
35	//
36	//	[4B magic 0xCC1DA7A1][4B payload_len][payload][4B CRC32]
37	//
38	//	payload:
39	//	  [8B timestamp int64]
40	//	  [2B metric_name_len uint16][N metric name bytes]
41	//	  [1B selector_count uint8]
42	//	  per selector: [1B selector_len uint8][M selector bytes]
43	//	  [4B value float32 bits]
44	//
45	// # Binary Snapshot Format
46	//
47	//	[4B magic 0xCC5B0001][8B from int64][8B to int64]
48	//	Level tree (recursive):
49	//	  [4B num_metrics uint32]
50	//	  per metric:
51	//	    [2B name_len uint16][N name bytes]
52	//	    [8B frequency int64][8B start int64]
53	//	    [4B num_values uint32][num_values × 4B float32]
54	//	  [4B num_children uint32]
55	//	  per child: [2B name_len uint16][N name bytes] + Level (recursive)
56	package metricstore
57	
58	import (
59		"bufio"
60		"context"
61		"encoding/binary"
62		"fmt"
63		"hash/crc32"
64		"hash/fnv"
65		"io"
66		"math"
67		"os"
68		"path"
69		"strings"
70		"sync"
71		"sync/atomic"
72		"time"
73	
74		cclog "github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/ccLogger"
75		"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/schema"
76	)
77	
78	// Magic numbers for binary formats.
79	const (
80		walFileMagic   = uint32(0xCC1DA701) // WAL file header magic
81		walRecordMagic = uint32(0xCC1DA7A1) // WAL record magic
82		snapFileMagic  = uint32(0xCC5B0001) // Binary snapshot magic
83	)
84	
85	// walShardChs holds per-shard channels for WAL messages.
86	// Initialized by WALStaging; nil when WAL is not active.
87	var walShardChs []chan *WALMessage
88	
89	// walShardRotateChs holds per-shard channels for WAL rotation requests.
90	var walShardRotateChs []chan walRotateReq
91	
92	// walNumShards stores the number of shards (set during WALStaging init).
93	var walNumShards int
94	
95	// walStagingWg tracks WALStaging goroutine exits for shutdown synchronization.
96	var walStagingWg sync.WaitGroup
97	
98	// walShuttingDown is set before closing shard channels to prevent
99	// SendWALMessage from sending on a closed channel (which panics in Go).
100	var walShuttingDown atomic.Bool

100	var walShuttingDown atomic.Bool
101	
102	// WALMessage represents a single metric write to be appended to the WAL.
103	// Cluster and Node are NOT stored in the WAL record (inferred from file path).
104	type WALMessage struct {
105		MetricName string
106		Cluster    string
107		Node       string
108		Selector   []string
109		Value      schema.Float
110		Timestamp  int64
111	}
112	
113	// walRotateReq requests WAL file rotation for a specific host directory.
114	// The done channel is closed by the WAL goroutine when rotation is complete.
115	type walRotateReq struct {
116		hostDir string
117		done    chan struct{}
118	}
119	
120	// walFileState holds an open WAL file handle and buffered writer for one host directory.
121	type walFileState struct {
122		f     *os.File
123		w     *bufio.Writer
124		dirty bool
125		size  int64 // approximate bytes written (tracked from open + writes)
126	}
127	
128	// walFlushInterval controls how often dirty WAL files are flushed to disk.
129	// Decoupling flushes from message processing lets the consumer run at memory
130	// speed, amortizing syscall overhead across many writes.
131	const walFlushInterval = 1 * time.Second
132	
133	// walShardIndex computes which shard a message belongs to based on cluster+node.
134	// Uses FNV-1a hash for fast, well-distributed mapping.
135	func walShardIndex(cluster, node string) int {
136		h := fnv.New32a()
137		h.Write([]byte(cluster))
138		h.Write([]byte{0})
139		h.Write([]byte(node))
140		return int(h.Sum32()) % walNumShards
141	}
142	
143	// SendWALMessage routes a WAL message to the appropriate shard channel.
144	// Returns false if the channel is full or shutdown is in progress.
145	func SendWALMessage(msg *WALMessage) bool {
146		if walShardChs == nil || walShuttingDown.Load() {
147			return false
148		}
149		shard := walShardIndex(msg.Cluster, msg.Node)
150		select {
151		case walShardChs[shard] <- msg:
152			return true
153		default:
154			return false
155		}
156	}
157	
158	// WALStaging starts N sharded background goroutines that receive WALMessage items
159	// and append binary WAL records to per-host current.wal files.
160	// Each shard owns a subset of hosts (determined by hash of cluster+node),
161	// parallelizing serialization and I/O while keeping per-host writes serial.
162	func WALStaging(wg *sync.WaitGroup, ctx context.Context) {
163		if Keys.Checkpoints.FileFormat == "json" {
164			return
165		}
166	
167		walNumShards = max(Keys.NumWorkers, 1)
168		chBufSize := max(65536/walNumShards, 1024)
169	
170		walShardChs = make([]chan *WALMessage, walNumShards)
171		walShardRotateChs = make([]chan walRotateReq, walNumShards)
172	
173		for i := range walNumShards {
174			walShardChs[i] = make(chan *WALMessage, chBufSize)
175			walShardRotateChs[i] = make(chan walRotateReq, 64)
176		}
177	
178		for i := range walNumShards {
179			msgCh := walShardChs[i]
180			rotateCh := walShardRotateChs[i]
181	
182			walStagingWg.Add(1)
183			wg.Go(func() {
184				defer walStagingWg.Done()
185				hostFiles := make(map[string]*walFileState)
186	
187				defer func() {
188					for _, ws := range hostFiles {
189						if ws.f != nil {
190							ws.w.Flush()
191							ws.f.Close()
192						}
193					}
194				}()
195	
196				getOrOpenWAL := func(hostDir string) *walFileState {
197					ws, ok := hostFiles[hostDir]
198					if ok {
199						return ws

content.text: I now have a clear understanding of the issue. Let me write the plan.

input.content:
# Fix: WAL rotation "channel full" warnings for all nodes

## Context

After every checkpoint, **all** nodes log `WAL rotation skipped for ... (channel full)`. This means WAL files are never rotated (deleted after a successful binary snapshot), causing unbounded WAL file growth — effectively defeating the purpose of checkpointing.

## Root Cause

In `pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go`:

1. **Rotation channels are small** (line 175): `walShardRotateChs[i] = make(chan walRotateReq, 64)` — buffer of 64 per shard.
2. **Shards are few** (line 167): `walNumShards = max(Keys.NumWorkers, 1)` — typically 5-10 shards.
3. **Hundreds of hosts per shard**: A large cluster like "fritz" has many hundreds of nodes. With ~10 shards, each shard handles ~100+ hosts.
4. **Non-blocking send** (lines 365-371): `RotateWALFiles` uses `select { case ch <- req: ... default: skip }`. It sends all rotation requests in a tight loop without waiting.
5. **Slow consumer**: The WAL staging goroutine processes one rotation at a time (flush + close + delete = I/O), interleaved with incoming metric messages.

Result: The first 64 rotation requests per shard succeed (fill the buffer), then every subsequent host is skipped with the warning. Since the staging goroutine can't drain 64 I/O-bound rotations fast enough during the tight loop, nearly all nodes are skipped.

## Fix

**Change the non-blocking send to a blocking send with a timeout** in `RotateWALFiles` (line 365-371).

The checkpoint goroutine is a background task — it can afford to wait. This ensures all hosts get their WAL rotated.

### File to modify

`pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go` — `RotateWALFiles` function (lines 357-381)

### Change

Replace the non-blocking `select` with a blocking send + timeout:

```go
func RotateWALFiles(hostDirs []string) {
	if walShardRotateChs == nil || walShuttingDown.Load() {
		return
	}
	dones := make([]chan struct{}, 0, len(hostDirs))
	for _, dir := range hostDirs {
		done := make(chan struct{})
		shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
		select {
		case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
			dones = append(dones, done)
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out for %s", dir)
		}
	}
	for _, done := range dones {
		select {
		case <-done:
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warn("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation timed out, continuing")
			return
		}
	}
}
```

Key change: `default:` (non-blocking skip) becomes `case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):` (blocking with timeout). This allows the staging goroutine time to drain the channel.

**Note**: Using `time.After` in a loop over potentially thousands of hosts creates a timer per iteration. Since this runs once per checkpoint interval (not a hot path), this is acceptable. If preferred, a single shared deadline can be used instead:

```go
deadline := time.After(2 * time.Minute) // single deadline for all sends
for _, dir := range hostDirs {
    done := make(chan struct{})
    shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
    select {
    case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
        dones = append(dones, done)
    case <-deadline:
        cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out after deadline, %d hosts remaining", len(hostDirs)-len(dones))
        goto waitDones
    }
}
```

I'd recommend the single-deadline approach for cleanliness.

## Verification

1. Build: `go build ./pkg/metricstore/...`
2. Run tests: `go test ./pkg/metricstore/...`
3. Deploy and observe: after checkpoint, no more "channel full" warnings; WAL files should be deleted for all hosts after each successful checkpoint.

content.text: Good — with thousands of nodes, the issue is even more acute. Let me refine the plan slightly.

input.old_string:
I'd recommend the single-deadline approach for cleanliness.

## Verification
input.new_string:
I'd recommend the single-deadline approach for cleanliness.

### Additional consideration: rotation starvation under load

The WAL staging goroutine's `drainBatch()` processes up to 4096 messages before yielding back to the main `select` (which can then pick a rotation request). With thousands of nodes continuously sending metrics, the rotation channel may drain slowly because the goroutine alternates between large message batches and individual rotations.

With a blocking send + 2-minute deadline this should still work (each rotation is fast I/O — flush/close/delete), but if it proves tight, we could also process pending rotation requests inside `drainBatch()`. For now, the blocking-send fix alone should resolve the issue.

## Verification

+### Additional consideration: rotation starvation under load

+The WAL staging goroutine's `drainBatch()` processes up to 4096 messages before yielding back to the main `select` (which can then pick a rotation request). With thousands of nodes continuously sending metrics, the rotation channel may drain slowly because the goroutine alternates between large message batches and individual rotations.

content.text:
The root cause is clear: `RotateWALFiles` uses a **non-blocking send** (`default:` case) on rotation channels buffered at only 64. With thousands of nodes and ~10 shards, the tight loop fills each shard's buffer instantly, and every remaining host is skipped. WAL files are never deleted.

The fix is simple: change the non-blocking send to a **blocking send with a timeout**, so the checkpoint goroutine waits for the staging goroutine to drain the channel instead of immediately giving up.

input.plan:
# Fix: WAL rotation "channel full" warnings for all nodes

## Context

After every checkpoint, **all** nodes log `WAL rotation skipped for ... (channel full)`. This means WAL files are never rotated (deleted after a successful binary snapshot), causing unbounded WAL file growth — effectively defeating the purpose of checkpointing.

## Root Cause

In `pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go`:

1. **Rotation channels are small** (line 175): `walShardRotateChs[i] = make(chan walRotateReq, 64)` — buffer of 64 per shard.
2. **Shards are few** (line 167): `walNumShards = max(Keys.NumWorkers, 1)` — typically 5-10 shards.
3. **Hundreds of hosts per shard**: A large cluster like "fritz" has many hundreds of nodes. With ~10 shards, each shard handles ~100+ hosts.
4. **Non-blocking send** (lines 365-371): `RotateWALFiles` uses `select { case ch <- req: ... default: skip }`. It sends all rotation requests in a tight loop without waiting.
5. **Slow consumer**: The WAL staging goroutine processes one rotation at a time (flush + close + delete = I/O), interleaved with incoming metric messages.

Result: The first 64 rotation requests per shard succeed (fill the buffer), then every subsequent host is skipped with the warning. Since the staging goroutine can't drain 64 I/O-bound rotations fast enough during the tight loop, nearly all nodes are skipped.

## Fix

**Change the non-blocking send to a blocking send with a timeout** in `RotateWALFiles` (line 365-371).

The checkpoint goroutine is a background task — it can afford to wait. This ensures all hosts get their WAL rotated.

### File to modify

`pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go` — `RotateWALFiles` function (lines 357-381)

### Change

Replace the non-blocking `select` with a blocking send + timeout:

```go
func RotateWALFiles(hostDirs []string) {
	if walShardRotateChs == nil || walShuttingDown.Load() {
		return
	}
	dones := make([]chan struct{}, 0, len(hostDirs))
	for _, dir := range hostDirs {
		done := make(chan struct{})
		shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
		select {
		case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
			dones = append(dones, done)
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out for %s", dir)
		}
	}
	for _, done := range dones {
		select {
		case <-done:
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warn("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation timed out, continuing")
			return
		}
	}
}
```

Key change: `default:` (non-blocking skip) becomes `case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):` (blocking with timeout). This allows the staging goroutine time to drain the channel.

**Note**: Using `time.After` in a loop over potentially thousands of hosts creates a timer per iteration. Since this runs once per checkpoint interval (not a hot path), this is acceptable. If preferred, a single shared deadline can be used instead:

```go
deadline := time.After(2 * time.Minute) // single deadline for all sends
for _, dir := range hostDirs {
    done := make(chan struct{})
    shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
    select {
    case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
        dones = append(dones, done)
    case <-deadline:
        cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out after deadline, %d hosts remaining", len(hostDirs)-len(dones))
        goto waitDones
    }
}
```

I'd recommend the single-deadline approach for cleanliness.

### Additional consideration: rotation starvation under load

The WAL staging goroutine's `drainBatch()` processes up to 4096 messages before yielding back to the main `select` (which can then pick a rotation request). With thousands of nodes continuously sending metrics, the rotation channel may drain slowly because the goroutine alternates between large message batches and individual rotations.

With a blocking send + 2-minute deadline this should still work (each rotation is fast I/O — flush/close/delete), but if it proves tight, we could also process pending rotation requests inside `drainBatch()`. For now, the blocking-send fix alone should resolve the issue.

## Verification

1. Build: `go build ./pkg/metricstore/...`
2. Run tests: `go test ./pkg/metricstore/...`
3. Deploy and observe: after checkpoint, no more "channel full" warnings; WAL files should be deleted for all hosts after each successful checkpoint.

## Context

## Root Cause

In `pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go`:

1. **Rotation channels are small** (line 175): `walShardRotateChs[i] = make(chan walRotateReq, 64)` — buffer of 64 per shard.
2. **Shards are few** (line 167): `walNumShards = max(Keys.NumWorkers, 1)` — typically 5-10 shards.
3. **Hundreds of hosts per shard**: A large cluster like "fritz" has many hundreds of nodes. With ~10 shards, each shard handles ~100+ hosts.
4. **Non-blocking send** (lines 365-371): `RotateWALFiles` uses `select { case ch <- req: ... default: skip }`. It sends all rotation requests in a tight loop without waiting.
5. **Slow consumer**: The WAL staging goroutine processes one rotation at a time (flush + close + delete = I/O), interleaved with incoming metric messages.

Result: The first 64 rotation requests per shard succeed (fill the buffer), then every subsequent host is skipped with the warning. Since the staging goroutine can't drain 64 I/O-bound rotations fast enough during the tight loop, nearly all nodes are skipped.

## Fix

**Change the non-blocking send to a blocking send with a timeout** in `RotateWALFiles` (line 365-371).

The checkpoint goroutine is a background task — it can afford to wait. This ensures all hosts get their WAL rotated.

### File to modify

`pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go` — `RotateWALFiles` function (lines 357-381)

### Change

Replace the non-blocking `select` with a blocking send + timeout:

```go
func RotateWALFiles(hostDirs []string) {
	if walShardRotateChs == nil || walShuttingDown.Load() {
		return
	}
	dones := make([]chan struct{}, 0, len(hostDirs))
	for _, dir := range hostDirs {
		done := make(chan struct{})
		shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
		select {
		case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
			dones = append(dones, done)
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out for %s", dir)
		}
	}
	for _, done := range dones {
		select {
		case <-done:
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warn("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation timed out, continuing")
			return
		}
	}
}
```

Key change: `default:` (non-blocking skip) becomes `case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):` (blocking with timeout). This allows the staging goroutine time to drain the channel.

**Note**: Using `time.After` in a loop over potentially thousands of hosts creates a timer per iteration. Since this runs once per checkpoint interval (not a hot path), this is acceptable. If preferred, a single shared deadline can be used instead:

```go
deadline := time.After(2 * time.Minute) // single deadline for all sends
for _, dir := range hostDirs {
    done := make(chan struct{})
    shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
    select {
    case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
        dones = append(dones, done)
    case <-deadline:
        cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out after deadline, %d hosts remaining", len(hostDirs)-len(dones))
        goto waitDones
    }
}
```

I'd recommend the single-deadline approach for cleanliness.

### Additional consideration: rotation starvation under load

The WAL staging goroutine's `drainBatch()` processes up to 4096 messages before yielding back to the main `select` (which can then pick a rotation request). With thousands of nodes continuously sending metrics, the rotation channel may drain slowly because the goroutine alternates between large message batches and individual rotations.

With a blocking send + 2-minute deadline this should still work (each rotation is fast I/O — flush/close/delete), but if it proves tight, we could also process pending rotation requests inside `drainBatch()`. For now, the blocking-send fix alone should resolve the issue.

## Verification

1. Build: `go build ./pkg/metricstore/...`
2. Run tests: `go test ./pkg/metricstore/...`
3. Deploy and observe: after checkpoint, no more "channel full" warnings; WAL files should be deleted for all hosts after each successful checkpoint.

toolUseResult.plan:
# Fix: WAL rotation "channel full" warnings for all nodes

## Context

After every checkpoint, **all** nodes log `WAL rotation skipped for ... (channel full)`. This means WAL files are never rotated (deleted after a successful binary snapshot), causing unbounded WAL file growth — effectively defeating the purpose of checkpointing.

## Root Cause

In `pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go`:

1. **Rotation channels are small** (line 175): `walShardRotateChs[i] = make(chan walRotateReq, 64)` — buffer of 64 per shard.
2. **Shards are few** (line 167): `walNumShards = max(Keys.NumWorkers, 1)` — typically 5-10 shards.
3. **Hundreds of hosts per shard**: A large cluster like "fritz" has many hundreds of nodes. With ~10 shards, each shard handles ~100+ hosts.
4. **Non-blocking send** (lines 365-371): `RotateWALFiles` uses `select { case ch <- req: ... default: skip }`. It sends all rotation requests in a tight loop without waiting.
5. **Slow consumer**: The WAL staging goroutine processes one rotation at a time (flush + close + delete = I/O), interleaved with incoming metric messages.

Result: The first 64 rotation requests per shard succeed (fill the buffer), then every subsequent host is skipped with the warning. Since the staging goroutine can't drain 64 I/O-bound rotations fast enough during the tight loop, nearly all nodes are skipped.

## Fix

**Change the non-blocking send to a blocking send with a timeout** in `RotateWALFiles` (line 365-371).

The checkpoint goroutine is a background task — it can afford to wait. This ensures all hosts get their WAL rotated.

### File to modify

`pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go` — `RotateWALFiles` function (lines 357-381)

### Change

Replace the non-blocking `select` with a blocking send + timeout:

```go
func RotateWALFiles(hostDirs []string) {
	if walShardRotateChs == nil || walShuttingDown.Load() {
		return
	}
	dones := make([]chan struct{}, 0, len(hostDirs))
	for _, dir := range hostDirs {
		done := make(chan struct{})
		shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
		select {
		case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
			dones = append(dones, done)
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out for %s", dir)
		}
	}
	for _, done := range dones {
		select {
		case <-done:
		case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):
			cclog.Warn("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation timed out, continuing")
			return
		}
	}
}
```

Key change: `default:` (non-blocking skip) becomes `case <-time.After(30 * time.Second):` (blocking with timeout). This allows the staging goroutine time to drain the channel.

**Note**: Using `time.After` in a loop over potentially thousands of hosts creates a timer per iteration. Since this runs once per checkpoint interval (not a hot path), this is acceptable. If preferred, a single shared deadline can be used instead:

```go
deadline := time.After(2 * time.Minute) // single deadline for all sends
for _, dir := range hostDirs {
    done := make(chan struct{})
    shard := walShardIndexFromDir(dir)
    select {
    case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
        dones = append(dones, done)
    case <-deadline:
        cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out after deadline, %d hosts remaining", len(hostDirs)-len(dones))
        goto waitDones
    }
}
```

I'd recommend the single-deadline approach for cleanliness.

### Additional consideration: rotation starvation under load

The WAL staging goroutine's `drainBatch()` processes up to 4096 messages before yielding back to the main `select` (which can then pick a rotation request). With thousands of nodes continuously sending metrics, the rotation channel may drain slowly because the goroutine alternates between large message batches and individual rotations.

With a blocking send + 2-minute deadline this should still work (each rotation is fast I/O — flush/close/delete), but if it proves tight, we could also process pending rotation requests inside `drainBatch()`. For now, the blocking-send fix alone should resolve the issue.

## Verification

1. Build: `go build ./pkg/metricstore/...`
2. Run tests: `go test ./pkg/metricstore/...`
3. Deploy and observe: after checkpoint, no more "channel full" warnings; WAL files should be deleted for all hosts after each successful checkpoint.

content.text: Now let me implement the fix.

content.content: The file /Users/jan/prg/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend/pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go has been updated successfully.

lines: case walShardRotateChs[shard] <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: done}:
-      // Channel full or goroutine not consuming — skip this host.
-      cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation skipped for %s (channel full)", dir)
+      cclog.Warnf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation send timed out, %d of %d hosts remaining",
-      cclog.Warn("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation timed out, continuing")
+      cclog.Warn("[METRICSTORE]> WAL rotation completion timed out, continuing")

content.text:
Builds and tests pass. Here's what changed:

**`pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go` — `RotateWALFiles`**:
- **Before**: Non-blocking send (`default:` skip) — instantly gives up when the 64-slot channel buffer is full, skipping nearly all nodes on large clusters.
- **After**: Blocking send with a shared 2-minute deadline — waits for the staging goroutine to drain the channel, ensuring all hosts get their WAL rotated. If the deadline expires (shouldn't happen under normal conditions), it logs a warning with the count of remaining hosts and proceeds to wait for already-submitted rotations.

input.command:
git add pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix: WAL rotation skipped for all nodes due to non-blocking send on small channel

RotateWALFiles used a non-blocking send (select/default) on rotation
channels buffered at 64. With thousands of nodes and few shards, the
channel fills instantly and nearly all hosts are skipped, leaving WAL
files unrotated indefinitely.

Replace with a blocking send using a shared 2-minute deadline so the
checkpoint goroutine waits for the staging goroutine to drain the
channel instead of immediately giving up.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
